channel.title: TXID News
channel.description: Bitcoin, Macro Economics & Politics — Independent Analysis

item.title: Crypto Finally Has a Rulebook: What the SEC-CFTC Joint Guidance Means
item.description: On March 17, the SEC and CFTC issued their first-ever joint guidance classifying 16 cryptocurrencies as digital commodities — not securities. Combined with the Senate's 89-10 CBDC ban, the US regulatory landscape has shifted more in one week than in the previous five years.

item.title: Fed Holds, Bitcoin Waits: How Oil-Driven Inflation Is Reshaping the Macro Landscape
item.description: The Fed held rates at 3.50-3.75% on March 18, slashing its 2026 rate cut forecast to just one. With the Iran conflict pushing oil above $108 and PCE inflation revised to 2.7%, the liquidity catalyst Bitcoin bulls were counting on has been delayed indefinitely.

item.title: Why Bitcoin, Not Crypto
item.description: Bitcoin and 'crypto' are not the same thing. This distinction isn't tribal — it's structural. Here's why this publication covers Bitcoin exclusively, and why that matters for serious analysis.

item.title: Square's Default-On Lightning: The Biggest Bitcoin Payments Milestone Since El Salvador
item.description: Starting March 30, Block's Square will enable Bitcoin Lightning payments by default across 4 million merchants worldwide. Zero fees through 2026, instant settlement, no chargebacks. This is the largest single merchant onboarding event in Lightning Network history.

item.title: The Halving Aftermath: One Year of Reduced Supply
item.description: April 2025's fourth halving cut the block reward from 6.25 to 3.125 BTC. One year in, the supply shock is measurable: 164,250 fewer BTC created than the prior year. Miners adapted, price responded, and the network barely noticed.

item.title: The Lightning Economy Is Already Here
item.description: While skeptics debate whether Lightning 'works,' a parallel economy is quietly operating on it. Nostr zaps, Podcasting 2.0 streaming sats, merchant payments in El Salvador, and cross-border remittances — the Lightning economy is not theoretical. It's running.

item.title: Japan's YCC Exit and the Global Liquidity Squeeze
item.description: The Bank of Japan officially abandoned yield curve control in January 2026, letting 10-year JGB yields rise to 1.8%. The resulting yen carry trade unwind is draining global liquidity — and Bitcoin is caught in the crossflow.

item.title: EU's MiCA Enforcement Begins: Winners, Losers, and Unintended Consequences
item.description: The Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation is now fully enforced across all 27 EU member states. Three months in, the effects are visible: compliant exchanges are gaining share, Tether faces an existential European challenge, and DeFi protocols are routing around the rules entirely.

item.title: Bitcoin Hashrate Hits 800 EH/s: What It Means for Network Security
item.description: Bitcoin's hashrate crossed 800 exahashes per second for the first time on March 18, doubling from 400 EH/s just eighteen months ago. Behind the headline number lies a structural shift in mining geography, hardware efficiency, and what it means for Bitcoin's security model.

item.title: Dollar Milkshake Theory in 2026: Is DXY Divergence Bullish for Bitcoin?
item.description: The US Dollar Index has climbed to 108 as global capital flows toward US assets amid geopolitical uncertainty. Brent Santiago's 'milkshake theory' predicted this — but its implications for Bitcoin are more nuanced than bulls or bears admit.

item.title: US Strategic Bitcoin Reserve: Executive Order 14178 Six Months In
item.description: Six months after President Trump signed Executive Order 14178 establishing the Strategic Bitcoin Reserve, the US government holds approximately 207,000 BTC from seizures. The policy is clear: never sell. The debate is whether to buy more — and how to pay for it.

item.title: Self-Custody Is Not Optional
item.description: From Mt. Gox to FTX, the lesson is always the same: if you don't hold the keys, you don't hold the Bitcoin. After $25 billion in cumulative exchange losses, treating self-custody as an advanced feature rather than the default is indefensible.
